stem: The normal internal carotid artery (ICA) waveform demonstrates:
options: Low-resistance flow with continuous forward diastolic flow
High-resistance flow with reversed diastolic flow
rationale: The ICA supplies the low-resistance intracranial vascular bed, resulting in a low-resistance waveform with continuous forward diastolic flow. This differs from the high-resistance ECA waveform.

stem: The external carotid artery (ECA) is identified by:
options: High-resistance waveform with oscillations during temporal tap
rationale: The ECA supplies the high-resistance muscular bed of the face and scalp, showing a sharp systolic peak with little diastolic flow. The temporal tap test produces saw-tooth oscillations in the ECA waveform, confirming its identity.

stem: According to the Society of Radiologists in Ultrasound consensus, ICA stenosis of 50-69% is characterized by PSV:
rationale: SRU consensus criteria: 50-69% ICA stenosis shows PSV 125-230 cm/s, EDV 40-100 cm/s, and ICA/CCA PSV ratio 2-4. These hemodynamic criteria correlate with angiographic stenosis.

stem: Near-total ICA occlusion ('string sign') on ultrasound demonstrates:
options: Markedly reduced velocities with a thin trickle of color flow in a severely narrowed lumen
Very high peak systolic velocity >400 cm/s
rationale: Near-total occlusion paradoxically shows low or barely detectable velocities (not high) because the residual lumen is so small that very little flow passes through. Power Doppler may help detect the trickle of remaining flow.

stem: Complete ICA occlusion is diagnosed when:
options: No flow is detected in the ICA lumen with color, power, and spectral Doppler
Flow reversal is seen in the CCA
rationale: Total ICA occlusion shows an echogenic-filled lumen with absent color and spectral Doppler flow. The ipsilateral CCA may show a high-resistance 'internalized' ECA pattern. Contralateral ICA may show compensatory increased velocity.

stem: Carotid plaque that is predominantly hypoechoic (echolucent) is considered:
options: Unstable and associated with higher risk of embolic events
rationale: Hypoechoic (echolucent) plaque contains lipid, hemorrhage, or thrombus and is considered 'vulnerable' or unstable with higher embolic potential. Echogenic/calcified plaque is more stable but may shadow and obscure stenosis assessment.

stem: The vertebral artery normally demonstrates:
options: Antegrade flow with a low-resistance waveform similar to the ICA
rationale: The vertebral artery supplies the posterior cerebral circulation (low-resistance bed), so it shows continuous forward diastolic flow similar to the ICA. It is evaluated between the transverse processes of the cervical vertebrae.

stem: Subclavian steal syndrome causes the ipsilateral vertebral artery to show:
options: Reversed (retrograde) flow, especially during arm exercise
rationale: In subclavian steal, proximal subclavian artery stenosis/occlusion causes retrograde flow in the ipsilateral vertebral artery to supply the arm. Partial steal shows alternating or dampened flow; complete steal shows fully reversed flow.

stem: The carotid bulb is located at the:
options: Distal common carotid artery where it widens before bifurcating into the ICA and ECA
Origin of the CCA from the aorta
rationale: The carotid bulb is the widened portion at the distal CCA/proximal ICA at the bifurcation. It is a common site for atherosclerotic plaque formation. Flow separation and reversal along its posterior wall are normal hemodynamic phenomena.

stem: The ankle-brachial index (ABI) is calculated by:
options: Dividing the highest ankle systolic pressure by the highest brachial systolic pressure
Dividing brachial pressure by ankle pressure
rationale: ABI = highest ankle systolic pressure (DP or PT) / highest brachial systolic pressure. Normal ABI is 1.0-1.3. ABI 0.9-1.0 is borderline. ABI <0.9 indicates PAD. ABI >1.3 suggests non-compressible calcified vessels (e.g., diabetes).

stem: An ABI of 0.5 indicates:
rationale: ABI classification: 1.0-1.3 normal, 0.9-1.0 borderline, 0.7-0.9 mild PAD, 0.4-0.7 moderate PAD (claudication), <0.4 severe PAD (rest pain, tissue loss). ABI of 0.5 falls in the moderate-severe range.

stem: Normal lower extremity arterial waveforms demonstrate:
options: Triphasic pattern with sharp systolic peak, brief reversed flow, and low forward diastolic flow
rationale: Normal peripheral arteries show triphasic flow: rapid systolic upstroke (forward flow), brief early diastolic reversal (elastic recoil), and low-amplitude late diastolic forward flow. This reflects high peripheral resistance at rest.

stem: A monophasic waveform in a lower extremity artery indicates:
options: Significant proximal arterial stenosis or occlusion
rationale: Monophasic (low-resistance, dampened) waveforms result from proximal stenosis or occlusion causing loss of the normal triphasic pattern. The waveform becomes tardus-parvus: slow systolic rise and reduced peak velocity.

stem: Tardus-parvus waveform is characterized by:
options: Delayed systolic upstroke (tardus) and diminished peak velocity (parvus)
Sharp systolic peak with high velocity
rationale: Tardus (slow) parvus (small) describes a dampened waveform distal to a hemodynamically significant stenosis: prolonged acceleration time, rounded systolic peak, and reduced peak velocity. Indicates >50% proximal obstruction.

stem: Segmental pressures in arterial testing: a pressure gradient >20 mmHg between adjacent levels indicates:
options: Significant arterial stenosis or occlusion at or above the level of the drop
rationale: A pressure drop of >20 mmHg (some labs use >30 mmHg) between consecutive levels (high thigh to above knee, above knee to below knee, etc.) localizes significant arterial obstruction to that segment.

stem: Exercise testing in PAD evaluation is useful when:
options: Resting ABI is normal or borderline in a patient with claudication symptoms
rationale: Exercise (treadmill) testing unmasks hemodynamically significant disease in patients with normal resting ABI but exertional symptoms. ABI drops of >20% after exercise or failure to recover within 3 minutes are abnormal.

stem: A popliteal artery aneurysm is defined as a popliteal artery diameter greater than:
rationale: Popliteal artery aneurysm is defined as diameter >2 cm or >50% larger than the normal popliteal artery. They are the most common peripheral artery aneurysm and are frequently bilateral (50-70%). Associated with AAA in 30-50% of cases.

stem: The gold standard criterion for diagnosing deep vein thrombosis (DVT) on ultrasound is:
rationale: Non-compressibility is the primary and most reliable criterion. A normal vein collapses completely with gentle transducer pressure. Inability to completely compress the vein walls indicates DVT, even if thrombus is anechoic.

stem: Causes of upper extremity DVT include:
options: Central venous catheters, Paget-Schroetter syndrome, and malignancy
rationale: Upper extremity DVT is most commonly caused by central venous catheters (indwelling lines, ports). Paget-Schroetter syndrome is effort-related thrombosis in the subclavian/axillary vein. Malignancy and thoracic outlet syndrome are other causes.

stem: The subclavian vein cannot be compressed because:
options: It runs behind the clavicle which prevents direct transducer compression
It is an artery, not a vein
rationale: The subclavian vein passes posterior to the clavicle, making compression impossible. Diagnosis relies on spectral Doppler (loss of phasicity, absent augmentation), color fill, and comparison to the contralateral side.

stem: Normal venous spectral Doppler demonstrates:
options: Phasic flow that varies with respiration
rationale: Normal venous flow is phasic with respiration: flow increases with expiration (abdominal veins) or with inspiration (peripheral veins directed toward the heart). Loss of respiratory phasicity suggests proximal obstruction.

stem: Paget-Schroetter syndrome is:
options: Effort-related thrombosis of the axillary-subclavian vein, often in athletes
Arterial thrombosis of the subclavian artery
Chronic venous insufficiency of the leg
rationale: Paget-Schroetter (effort thrombosis) occurs in the axillary-subclavian vein due to repetitive arm motion (swimmers, pitchers, weightlifters) or thoracic outlet compression. Presents with acute arm swelling, cyanosis, and heaviness.

stem: Venous thoracic outlet syndrome involves compression of the:
options: Subclavian vein between the clavicle and first rib
Brachial vein in the antecubital fossa
Axillary artery by the pectoralis muscle
rationale: Venous TOS compresses the subclavian vein in the costoclavicular space between the clavicle and first rib. Provocative maneuvers (arm abduction, hyperabduction) may demonstrate flow reduction or occlusion during dynamic ultrasound.

stem: Central venous catheter-related thrombosis is best evaluated by:
options: Ultrasound of the catheterized vein assessing compressibility, flow, and thrombus around the catheter
rationale: Ultrasound evaluates for thrombus formation around the catheter (fibrin sheath, mural thrombus), vein compressibility, spectral flow patterns, and comparison with the contralateral side. The catheter itself appears as parallel echogenic lines.

stem: The normal renal artery Doppler waveform shows:
options: Low-resistance flow with continuous diastolic forward flow
rationale: The renal artery supplies the low-resistance renal vascular bed, producing continuous forward diastolic flow similar to the ICA. The normal renal artery RI is typically <0.70.

stem: The normal peak systolic velocity of the main renal artery is:
rationale: Normal main renal artery PSV is typically <180 cm/s. PSV >180-200 cm/s suggests ≥60% renal artery stenosis. The renal-to-aortic ratio (RAR) >3.5 also indicates significant stenosis.

stem: The renal-to-aortic ratio (RAR) for diagnosing significant renal artery stenosis is:
rationale: RAR = renal artery PSV / aortic PSV. RAR >3.5 indicates ≥60% renal artery stenosis. This ratio helps normalize for cardiac output variations. Combined with direct PSV criteria, it improves diagnostic accuracy.

stem: Tardus-parvus waveform in the intrarenal (segmental/interlobar) arteries suggests:
rationale: An intrarenal tardus-parvus waveform (prolonged acceleration time >0.07 seconds, acceleration index <3 m/s²) is an indirect sign of hemodynamically significant main renal artery stenosis proximal to the sampling site.

stem: An elevated renal resistive index (RI > 0.80) is associated with:
options: Medical renal disease, obstruction, or renal transplant rejection
rationale: Elevated RI (>0.70-0.80) reflects increased intrarenal vascular resistance from nephrosclerosis, diabetic nephropathy, acute tubular necrosis, obstruction, or transplant rejection. It is a nonspecific marker of parenchymal disease.

stem: Renal artery stenosis can cause:
options: Renovascular hypertension and progressive renal insufficiency
rationale: Renal artery stenosis activates the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, causing renovascular hypertension. Chronic bilateral stenosis can lead to ischemic nephropathy and progressive renal failure.

stem: Fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) of the renal artery most commonly affects:
options: Young to middle-aged women involving the mid to distal renal artery
Elderly men at the renal artery ostium
Post-menopausal women in the ICA only
rationale: FMD predominantly affects young women. The medial fibroplasia subtype (most common) creates a 'string of beads' appearance in the mid-distal renal artery. Unlike atherosclerosis which is ostial, FMD spares the proximal segment.

stem: The normal superior mesenteric artery (SMA) fasting waveform shows:
options: High-resistance flow with little or reversed diastolic flow
Low-resistance flow with high diastolic velocity
rationale: Fasting SMA has high-resistance flow because the mesenteric bed is in a resting state. After eating, the SMA converts to low-resistance flow with increased diastolic velocity as mesenteric vasodilation occurs for digestion.

stem: After a meal, the SMA waveform normally changes to:
options: Low-resistance flow with increased diastolic velocity
Higher resistance with reversed diastolic flow
rationale: Postprandial mesenteric vasodilation decreases resistance, increasing diastolic flow and total blood flow volume to the bowel by 50-100%. This is why mesenteric Doppler should be performed fasting for stenosis evaluation.

stem: Significant celiac artery stenosis is suggested by PSV greater than:
rationale: Celiac artery stenosis ≥70% shows PSV >200 cm/s and EDV >55 cm/s. The celiac artery supplies the liver, spleen, and stomach and normally has a low-resistance waveform even during fasting.

stem: Significant SMA stenosis is suggested by PSV greater than:
rationale: SMA stenosis ≥70% shows PSV >275 cm/s and EDV >45 cm/s. The SMA is best evaluated fasting to avoid postprandial changes that alter velocities. Retrograde flow in the common hepatic artery from the SMA suggests celiac occlusion.

stem: Median arcuate ligament syndrome (MALS) causes extrinsic compression of the:
rationale: MALS results from compression of the proximal celiac artery by the median arcuate ligament of the diaphragm. It typically shows elevated velocities that decrease with deep inspiration (which lifts the ligament). Expiratory velocities are higher.

stem: Chronic mesenteric ischemia typically requires stenosis or occlusion of at least:
options: Two of the three mesenteric vessels (celiac, SMA, IMA)
rationale: Due to extensive collateral pathways between the mesenteric arteries, chronic mesenteric ischemia symptoms (postprandial pain, weight loss, food fear) usually require ≥2 of 3 vessels to be significantly stenosed or occluded.

stem: A normal abdominal aorta diameter is:
rationale: Normal infrarenal abdominal aorta diameter is approximately 2.0 cm, with the upper limit of normal being 3.0 cm. Aneurysm is defined as dilation ≥3.0 cm or >50% increase from the expected normal diameter.

stem: Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) is defined as an aortic diameter of:
rationale: AAA is defined as infrarenal aortic diameter ≥3.0 cm (or >50% increase in diameter). Most AAAs are infrarenal (95%). Rupture risk increases significantly with diameter: <4 cm = <1%/year, 5-5.9 cm = 3-15%/year, ≥6 cm = 20-40%/year.

stem: Surgical or endovascular repair of AAA is typically recommended when the diameter reaches:
options: 5.5 cm in men, 5.0 cm in women
rationale: Current guidelines recommend elective repair at 5.5 cm for men and 5.0 cm for women, or if the growth rate exceeds 0.5 cm in 6 months. Symptomatic or ruptured aneurysms require emergent repair regardless of size.

stem: The most common location for AAA is:
rationale: Approximately 95% of AAAs are infrarenal. The infrarenal aorta is susceptible due to fewer vasa vasorum, absent elastic lamina in some areas, and hemodynamic factors. Measurement should include outer wall to outer wall in AP diameter.

stem: Mural thrombus in an AAA appears as:
options: Echogenic material lining the aortic wall with a smaller residual patent lumen
rationale: Mural thrombus lines the aneurysm wall, appearing as layered echogenic material. The true aneurysm diameter must include the thrombus (outer wall to outer wall), not just the residual patent lumen seen on color Doppler.

stem: AAA screening with ultrasound is recommended for:
options: Men aged 65-75 who have ever smoked
rationale: USPSTF recommends one-time screening ultrasonography for AAA in men aged 65-75 who have ever smoked. Selective screening may be considered for men who have never smoked. No routine screening recommendation for women.

stem: An aortic dissection on ultrasound may show:
options: An intimal flap within the aortic lumen creating true and false lumens
Only aortic dilation without intraluminal findings
rationale: Aortic dissection shows a mobile echogenic intimal flap dividing the lumen into true and false channels. Color Doppler may show different flow patterns in each lumen. CT angiography is the primary diagnostic tool; ultrasound may detect abdominal extension.

stem: Endoleak following endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR) is:
options: Persistent blood flow into the aneurysm sac outside the stent graft
Absence of flow in the graft
rationale: Endoleak is continued perfusion of the aneurysm sac outside the stent graft, maintaining rupture risk. Type I (attachment site), Type II (branch vessels, most common), Type III (graft defect), Type IV (graft porosity), Type V (endotension).

stem: Type II endoleak is caused by:
options: Retrograde flow from branch vessels (lumbar or inferior mesenteric arteries) into the sac
Inadequate seal at the proximal attachment site
rationale: Type II endoleak is the most common type, caused by retrograde flow from lumbar arteries or the IMA into the excluded aneurysm sac. Many are self-limiting but require surveillance. Intervention is needed if the sac expands.

stem: Inflammatory AAA is characterized by:
options: Thick hypoechoic rind around the anterior and lateral aortic wall
rationale: Inflammatory AAA (5-10% of AAAs) shows a thick (up to 1 cm) hypoechoic mantle of inflammatory tissue surrounding the anterior and lateral aortic wall. It may encase adjacent structures (ureters, duodenum). Associated with retroperitoneal fibrosis.

stem: A mycotic (infected) aortic aneurysm typically presents as:
options: Saccular aneurysm with irregular walls, periaortic fluid, and rapid expansion
rationale: Mycotic aneurysms are caused by infection (bacterial, typically Salmonella or Staphylococcus). They tend to be saccular, eccentric, with irregular/lobulated walls, periaortic fluid/gas, and rapidly enlarging. High rupture risk requires urgent treatment.

stem: The iliac arteries should be evaluated during AAA screening because:
options: Iliac aneurysms frequently coexist with AAA
They are always normal when AAA is present
They are not accessible by ultrasound
rationale: Iliac aneurysms accompany AAA in 20-40% of cases. Common iliac artery aneurysm is defined as >1.5-1.8 cm. The iliac arteries should be assessed for dilation, stenosis, and extension of aortic disease during screening.

stem: Bypass graft surveillance with duplex ultrasound is performed to:
options: Detect stenotic lesions within or adjacent to the graft before occlusion occurs
rationale: Post-bypass graft surveillance identifies developing stenoses (intimal hyperplasia, progression of native disease) while the graft is still patent, allowing preemptive intervention. Graft stenosis treated before occlusion has better outcomes.

stem: The most common cause of infrainguinal vein bypass graft failure within 2 years is:
options: Intimal hyperplasia at the anastomosis or within the graft
rationale: Intimal hyperplasia (neointimal fibroplasia) is the primary cause of graft stenosis within the first 2 years, occurring at anastomotic sites, valve sites, or within the body of vein grafts. After 2 years, native disease progression becomes more common.

stem: In bypass graft surveillance, a peak systolic velocity ratio (Vr) >3.5 across a lesion indicates:
rationale: Velocity ratio (PSV at stenosis / PSV proximal to stenosis) categorizes graft stenosis: Vr <2 is <50% stenosis (monitor), Vr 2-3.5 is 50-75% (close surveillance), Vr >3.5 is >75% (intervention recommended to prevent occlusion).

stem: Low graft flow velocity (<45 cm/s throughout the graft) suggests:
options: Failing graft with high risk of thrombosis
rationale: Uniformly low velocities (<45 cm/s) indicate poor graft flow (low-flow state), which predisposes to graft thrombosis. Causes include inflow stenosis, outflow disease, or diffuse graft narrowing. Close surveillance or intervention is warranted.

stem: Prosthetic (synthetic) bypass grafts differ from vein grafts in that they:
options: Have echogenic walls, show no valve sites, and are more prone to anastomotic stenosis
rationale: Prosthetic grafts (PTFE, Dacron) have characteristic corrugated echogenic walls. They lack valve sites but are more susceptible to compliance mismatch at anastomoses, leading to intimal hyperplasia. They also have lower long-term patency than vein grafts.

stem: Retained valve leaflets in in-situ saphenous vein bypass grafts cause:
options: Focal stenosis with elevated velocities at valve sites
rationale: In-situ vein grafts use the saphenous vein in place with valve cusps lysed. Retained or incompletely lysed valve leaflets cause focal stenosis with increased velocities at those sites, identified on surveillance duplex.

stem: Arteriovenous fistulae (AVF) in in-situ grafts are identified by:
options: High-velocity, low-resistance flow into a nearby vein with tissue vibration artifact
rationale: In-situ vein bypass requires ligation of tributary branches. Missed tributaries create AVFs with high diastolic flow, perivascular tissue vibration (color 'bruit'), and localized steal from the graft. Small AVFs may not require treatment.

stem: Pseudoaneurysm (PSA) at an anastomotic site appears on ultrasound as:
options: A pulsatile fluid collection outside the vessel communicating through a defect ('yin-yang' sign on color Doppler)
rationale: Anastomotic pseudoaneurysm appears as a contained extravascular collection with swirling blood flow ('yin-yang' or 'to-and-fro' pattern on color Doppler) communicating with the vessel through a neck. The 'to-and-fro' spectral pattern at the neck is diagnostic.

stem: Normal hepatic venous waveform demonstrates:
options: Pulsatile triphasic flow reflecting right atrial pressure changes
rationale: Hepatic veins show a triphasic pulsatile waveform: two antegrade (toward the heart) phases during atrial relaxation and ventricular systole, and one brief retrograde phase during atrial contraction (a-wave). Loss of pulsatility suggests liver disease.

stem: Portal vein flow in cirrhosis may demonstrate:
options: Hepatofugal (reversed) flow away from the liver
rationale: In advanced cirrhosis with portal hypertension, portal venous flow may become hepatofugal (reversed, away from the liver) through portosystemic collaterals. Decreased velocity (<16 cm/s), flattened waveform, or bidirectional flow may also occur.

stem: The normal direction of portal venous flow is:
rationale: Normal portal flow is hepatopetal—toward the liver—carrying nutrient-rich blood from the GI tract to the liver sinusoids. Normal velocity is 15-40 cm/s. Hepatofugal flow indicates portal hypertension with portosystemic shunting.

stem: TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) surveillance includes measuring:
options: Peak shunt velocity at the hepatic vein end, mid-shunt, and portal vein end
Only the main portal vein velocity
rationale: TIPS surveillance measures velocities at the portal venous end, mid-shunt, and hepatic venous end. Shunt stenosis is suggested by PSV <90 cm/s or >190 cm/s at any site, or temporal velocity change >50 cm/s from baseline.

stem: Renal transplant Doppler evaluation includes:
options: Main renal artery PSV, intrarenal RI, and assessment for stenosis, thrombosis, and collections
rationale: Renal transplant duplex evaluates the transplant renal artery (stenosis at anastomosis), intrarenal RI (elevated in rejection, ATN), renal vein (thrombosis), and perinephric collections (hematoma, lymphocele, urinoma).

stem: Transplant renal artery stenosis (TRAS) is suspected when:
options: PSV >250 cm/s at the anastomosis with turbulence and distal tardus-parvus waveforms
Absent diastolic flow in all vessels
rationale: TRAS typically occurs at the arterial anastomosis. PSV >250 cm/s at the anastomosis, velocity ratio >3:1 compared to the upstream iliac artery, and downstream tardus-parvus waveforms support the diagnosis.

stem: Hemodialysis AV fistula maturation criteria include:
options: Vein diameter ≥6 mm, flow volume ≥600 mL/min, depth ≤6 mm from skin surface
rationale: The 'Rule of 6s' for fistula maturation: diameter ≥6 mm, flow ≥600 mL/min, depth ≤6 mm, assessed at 6 weeks post-creation. Immature fistulas failing these criteria may need intervention or alternative access.

stem: Steal syndrome in a hemodialysis access presents with:
options: Ischemic hand symptoms (pain, coolness, pallor) due to arterial blood diverted into the fistula
Arm swelling proximal to the fistula
rationale: Steal syndrome occurs when the fistula diverts excessive arterial flow from the distal extremity. Doppler shows retrograde flow in the artery distal to the anastomosis. Symptoms range from coolness to digital ischemia/gangrene.

stem: The popliteal vein is best evaluated with the patient in which position?
options: Prone or with the knee slightly flexed in a dependent position
rationale: The popliteal vein is best assessed with the patient prone or decubitus with the knee slightly flexed. This allows optimal transducer access to the popliteal fossa and prevents vein compression from the examination table.

stem: Venous reflux testing using duplex ultrasound employs:
options: Distal augmentation with proximal compression release or Valsalva, measuring reflux duration
rationale: Reflux is assessed by augmenting flow then releasing compression or using Valsalva. Reflux duration >0.5 seconds in superficial veins or >1.0 second in deep veins is abnormal, indicating valvular incompetence and chronic venous insufficiency.

stem: The great saphenous vein (GSV) is located:
options: Medial leg and thigh, draining into the common femoral vein at the saphenofemoral junction
Posterior calf draining into the popliteal vein
rationale: The GSV runs along the medial leg and thigh superficially within the saphenous compartment. It joins the common femoral vein at the saphenofemoral junction (SFJ) in the groin. It is the most commonly refluxing vein in chronic venous insufficiency.

stem: The small saphenous vein (SSV) drains into:
options: The popliteal vein at the saphenopopliteal junction
rationale: The SSV courses along the posterior calf and typically drains into the popliteal vein at the saphenopopliteal junction (SPJ). The SPJ location is variable and should be mapped preoperatively. SSV reflux is an important cause of CVI.

stem: Perforator vein incompetence is significant because:
options: It allows transmission of deep venous pressure to the superficial system
It causes only deep vein thrombosis
It only occurs in the upper extremity
rationale: Perforator veins connect deep and superficial systems. Normal flow is deep (inward). Incompetent perforators allow outward flow, transmitting high deep venous pressure to the superficial veins, contributing to varicosities and venous ulcers.

stem: Acute DVT on ultrasound typically appears:
options: Hypoechoic/anechoic thrombus with a distended, non-compressible vein
Echogenic thrombus with a contracted vein
rationale: Acute thrombus is often anechoic or hypoechoic (may be nearly invisible on B-mode), making compression the essential test. The vein is distended by fresh clot. As thrombus ages, it becomes more echogenic and the vein contracts.

stem: Superficial venous thrombosis (SVT) is concerning when it is within:
rationale: SVT within 3 cm of the SFJ has significant risk of propagation into the deep venous system (CFV), potentially causing DVT and PE. Anticoagulation or close surveillance is recommended for SVT near the SFJ.

stem: Post-thrombotic syndrome (PTS) results from:
options: Chronic venous damage with valvular incompetence and outflow obstruction following DVT
rationale: PTS occurs in 20-50% of DVT patients. Chronic thrombus damages venous valves causing reflux, and residual obstruction impairs outflow. Manifestations include edema, skin changes, pain, and venous ulceration. Duplex shows reflux and residual obstruction.

stem: A Baker's cyst can mimic DVT because:
options: It may compress the popliteal vein or rupture, causing calf swelling similar to DVT
It is located in the deep veins
rationale: Baker's cyst (popliteal synovial cyst) is located in the popliteal fossa. When large, it can compress the popliteal vein causing venous stasis. A ruptured Baker's cyst causes acute calf pain and swelling mimicking DVT clinically.

stem: The Doppler angle for peripheral arterial studies should ideally be:
rationale: Doppler angle should be ≤60 degrees for accurate velocity measurement. At angles approaching 90°, cos θ approaches zero, causing unreliable velocity calculations. At 0°, accuracy is best but rarely achievable anatomically.

stem: Color Doppler aliasing in an artery typically indicates:
options: Elevated velocity at a stenotic site
rationale: Aliasing (color wrap-around) in an artery on color Doppler indicates velocity exceeding the Nyquist limit, typically at a stenosis. It serves as a useful visual cue to place the spectral gate for velocity measurement at that location.

stem: Popliteal entrapment syndrome occurs when:
options: The popliteal artery is compressed by an abnormal relationship with the gastrocnemius muscle
The popliteal vein is always involved
There is atherosclerotic plaque in the popliteal artery
rationale: Popliteal entrapment is a congenital anomaly where the popliteal artery courses abnormally in relation to the medial gastrocnemius muscle or popliteal fossa structures. Provocative maneuvers (active plantar flexion) demonstrate flow reduction.

stem: Adventitial cystic disease of the popliteal artery shows:
options: A cystic lesion within the adventitia of the artery causing luminal stenosis
rationale: Adventitial cystic disease is a rare condition with mucoid cyst formation in the arterial adventitia, causing extrinsic compression. Ultrasound shows an anechoic or hypoechoic cystic mass within the vessel wall with luminal narrowing.

stem: Waveform changes distal to a hemodynamically significant arterial stenosis include:
options: Loss of the reversed flow component, dampened peak velocity, and prolonged acceleration time
Increased peak velocity with sharp upstroke
rationale: Distal to a significant stenosis, the waveform loses its triphasic character: the reversed component disappears, the systolic upstroke becomes rounded (tardus), and peak velocity is reduced (parvus) with prolonged acceleration time.

stem: Pulse volume recordings (PVRs) are:
options: Plethysmographic waveforms reflecting volume changes in the limb with each cardiac cycle
rationale: PVRs use air-filled cuffs at multiple levels to detect limb volume changes with each pulse. Normal PVR has a sharp systolic peak and dicrotic notch. Dampened waveforms (loss of dicrotic notch, rounded peak) indicate proximal arterial disease.

stem: Toe pressures and toe-brachial index (TBI) are useful when:
options: ABI is falsely elevated due to non-compressible calcified vessels (diabetes, renal disease)
rationale: Digital arteries rarely calcify, making toe pressures reliable when tibial arteries are non-compressible (ABI >1.3). TBI ≥0.70 is normal. TBI <0.70 indicates PAD. Absolute toe pressure <30 mmHg suggests critical limb ischemia.

stem: The celiac artery normally supplies:
options: The liver (common hepatic artery), spleen (splenic artery), and stomach (left gastric artery)
The small bowel and ascending colon
rationale: The celiac trunk (axis) is the first major anterior aortic branch, dividing into the common hepatic artery, splenic artery, and left gastric artery. It supplies the foregut derivatives: liver, spleen, stomach, proximal duodenum, and pancreas.

stem: The SMA supplies:
options: The small intestine, ascending colon, and proximal transverse colon
rationale: The SMA is the second anterior aortic branch, supplying the midgut: small intestine (jejunum and ileum), cecum, ascending colon, and proximal two-thirds of the transverse colon.

stem: The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) supplies:
options: The distal transverse colon, descending colon, sigmoid colon, and proximal rectum
rationale: The IMA supplies the hindgut derivatives: distal transverse colon, descending colon, sigmoid colon, and upper rectum. It is the smallest of the three mesenteric arteries and is frequently difficult to visualize on ultrasound.

stem: In mesenteric Doppler, the patient should fast for at least:
rationale: Fasting for 8-12 hours ensures the mesenteric arteries are in a high-resistance resting state. Postprandial vasodilation would alter velocities and potentially mask stenosis or falsely suggest pathology.

stem: The 'meandering mesenteric artery' (arc of Riolan) is significant because:
options: It is a collateral pathway between SMA and IMA territories that enlarges with proximal occlusion
It is a normal variant with no clinical significance
It is only seen in children
rationale: The arc of Riolan (meandering mesenteric artery) is a collateral connecting the SMA and IMA circulations via the marginal artery. It enlarges when either vessel is occluded, maintaining bowel perfusion. Its presence suggests chronic mesenteric vessel disease.

stem: Renal vein thrombosis on ultrasound shows:
options: Enlarged kidney with echogenic thrombus in the renal vein and absent or reversed diastolic arterial flow
Small kidney with normal renal vein
rationale: Acute renal vein thrombosis causes venous outflow obstruction: enlarged edematous kidney, echogenic thrombus in the renal vein (extending into the IVC in some cases), and reversed or absent diastolic flow in the renal artery due to markedly elevated resistance.

stem: Nutcracker syndrome is caused by:
options: Compression of the left renal vein between the SMA and aorta
Compression of the right renal artery
rationale: Nutcracker syndrome (anterior) occurs when the left renal vein is compressed between the SMA and aorta. Doppler shows elevated velocity at the compression point with prestenotic dilation. Symptoms include hematuria, proteinuria, and left flank pain.

stem: A pseudoaneurysm at a femoral artery catheterization site is treated initially by:
options: Immediate surgical repair in all cases
rationale: Femoral pseudoaneurysms are common post-catheterization complications. Small PSAs (<2 cm) may thrombose spontaneously. Larger ones are treated with ultrasound-guided compression repair or, more commonly now, ultrasound-guided thrombin injection (>95% success rate).

stem: An arteriovenous fistula (AVF) at a catheterization site demonstrates:
options: High-velocity, low-resistance flow in the artery and arterialized pulsatile flow in the adjacent vein
rationale: Post-catheterization AVF shows a direct communication between artery and vein. The feeding artery has increased diastolic flow (low resistance), and the draining vein shows arterialized pulsatile flow. Perivascular tissue vibration artifact is characteristic.

stem: The proper technique for lower extremity venous compression ultrasound involves:
options: Applying gentle pressure with the transducer in the transverse plane to completely coapt the vein walls
Using color Doppler without any compression
Only checking at the groin level
rationale: Compression is performed in the transverse plane with firm but gentle pressure to completely collapse the vein. Compression every 1-2 cm from the CFV to the popliteal vein is the standard technique. Complete coaptation of walls excludes DVT at that site.

stem: May-Thurner syndrome involves:
options: Compression of the left common iliac vein by the right common iliac artery against the spine
Compression of the right iliac vein by the left iliac artery
rationale: May-Thurner syndrome (iliac vein compression syndrome) results from the right common iliac artery compressing the left common iliac vein against the lumbar spine, causing intimal changes, stenosis, and predisposition to left iliofemoral DVT.

stem: The proper Doppler angle for ICA velocity measurement should be:
options: 60 degrees or less, aligned parallel to the vessel wall (not the lumen center)
rationale: The Doppler angle should be ≤60° and aligned parallel to the vessel wall/direction of blood flow. At angles >60°, small angle errors produce large velocity errors. Angle correction should follow the flow direction, not the vessel center.

stem: Carotid intima-media thickness (CIMT) measurement is performed on the:
options: Far wall of the distal CCA in a longitudinal plane
rationale: CIMT is measured on the far wall of the distal CCA (1 cm proximal to the bulb) as the distance between the lumen-intima and media-adventitia interfaces. Increased CIMT (>0.9-1.0 mm) is a marker of subclinical atherosclerosis and cardiovascular risk.

stem: Bilateral high-resistance (internalized) CCA waveforms may indicate:
options: Bilateral ICA occlusion with the CCA supplying only the high-resistance ECA territory
rationale: When both ICAs are occluded, the CCAs supply only the high-resistance ECA territories. The CCA waveform loses its usual low-resistance diastolic flow component and resembles an ECA waveform bilaterally.

stem: During carotid duplex, the common carotid artery should be evaluated from:
options: Its origin/proximal portion through to the bifurcation
rationale: Complete evaluation includes the proximal CCA (or as low as possible), mid and distal CCA, bulb, proximal ICA/ECA, and vertebral arteries. Proximal CCA disease (especially left CCA from the aortic arch) can affect downstream hemodynamics.

stem: Carotid body tumor (paraganglioma) appears on duplex as:
options: A hypervascular mass at the carotid bifurcation splaying the ICA and ECA apart
A cystic lesion within the ICA
rationale: Carotid body tumors are highly vascular paragangliomas at the carotid bifurcation. They characteristically splay (widen the distance between) the ICA and ECA. Color Doppler shows intense vascularity within the mass.

stem: Carotid dissection on ultrasound may show:
options: Intimal flap, tapered narrowing, intramural hematoma (echogenic wall thickening), and altered flow patterns
rationale: Carotid dissection may demonstrate an intimal flap (if visible), eccentric wall thickening from intramural hematoma, luminal narrowing with dampened flow, or occlusion. CTA/MRA is the definitive imaging modality.

stem: The proper graft surveillance protocol includes duplex evaluation at:
options: 1 month, 3 months, 6 months, then every 6-12 months post-operatively
rationale: Graft surveillance typically follows: baseline within 30 days post-op, 3 months, 6 months, then every 6-12 months indefinitely. More frequent surveillance may be needed if abnormalities are detected. Early detection of stenosis preserves graft patency.

stem: An occluded bypass graft on duplex ultrasound shows:
options: Echogenic material filling the graft lumen with no color or spectral Doppler flow
Normal triphasic flow within the graft
High-velocity flow at the anastomosis only
rationale: Occluded grafts show an echogenic-filled lumen with absent color and spectral Doppler flow throughout. The inflow and outflow native arteries should be evaluated for potential alternative bypass or endovascular options.
